  2. Go to the Agents tab. You will see Translator Assistant loaded automatically (the file watcher picked it up instantly without a restart!).
  3. Select the agent, open the Chat Tester, and type: "Hello, what a beautiful day!"
  4. The agent will reply: "¡Hola, qué hermoso día!"

Enjoy building declarative, high-performance agent teams with Soulacy!

docs.title: Skills
docs.text: Skills are reusable capability bundles that combine a system prompt fragment, tool set, and configuration — packaged so they can be mixed into any agent.

docs.title: What is a skill?
docs.text:
A skill is a YAML fragment you can include in a SOUL.yaml file:

skills/web-researcher.skill.yaml
tools:
  - web_search
  - url_fetch
system_prompt_append: |
  When answering factual questions, search the web to find current information.
  Always cite your sources.

Apply it to an agent:

agents/assistant.soul.yaml
name: assistant
model: gpt-4o
system_prompt: You are a helpful assistant.
skills:
  - web-researcher
channels:
  - http

The skill's tools and prompt fragment are merged into the agent at load time.

docs.title: Built-in skills
docs.text: Skill Description web-researcher Web search + URL fetch with citation instructions code-helper Code interpreter + coding-focused prompt data-analyst Calculator + code interpreter + data analysis prompt image-reader Image analysis tool + vision prompt

docs.title: Creating a skill
docs.text:
Place a .skill.yaml file in your skills/ directory (or configure skills.dir in config):

skills/customer-support.skill.yaml
system_prompt_append: |
  You are a support agent for Acme Corp.
  - Only discuss Acme products.
  - Escalate billing issues to human support.
  - Be empathetic and concise.

tools: []  # no extra tools needed

Apply to multiple agents:

# agents/telegram-support.soul.yaml
name: telegram-support
model: gpt-4o-mini
system_prompt: You help Acme customers via Telegram.
skills:
  - customer-support
channels:
  - telegram

# agents/web-support.soul.yaml
name: web-support
model: gpt-4o-mini
system_prompt: You help Acme customers via the web widget.
skills:
  - customer-support
channels:
  - http

docs.title: Skill directory
docs.text:
config.yaml
agents:
  dir: ./agents
  skills_dir: ./skills   # default: ./skills

docs.title: Multiple skills
docs.text:
Skills are merged in order — later skills override earlier ones for conflicting fields:

skills:
  - web-researcher
  - customer-support   # this prompt append comes second

docs.title: SOUL.yaml Reference
docs.text: Every agent is defined by a *.soul.yaml file in your agents directory. This page documents every field.

docs.title: Complete schema
docs.text:
# ── Identity ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
name: my-agent                # required; used as the agent ID in URLs and logs
description: "What this agent does"
version: "1.0.0"              # optional; for marketplace/registry

# ── Model ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
model: gpt-4o-mini            # required
# Formats:
#   gpt-4o-mini                 → uses default_provider
#   anthropic/claude-3-5-haiku → explicit provider prefix
#   ollama/llama3.2            → local Ollama model

# ── Prompt ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
system_prompt: |
  You are a helpful assistant.

# ── Tools ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
tools:
  - web_search
  - url_fetch
  - calculator

# ── Token budget ──────────────────────────────────────────────
token_budget:
  max_input_tokens: 32000     # max context sent to LLM
  max_output_tokens: 1024     # max tokens in response

# ── Session / history ─────────────────────────────────────────
session:
  history_turns: 20           # prior turns to include in context

# ── Channels ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
channels:
  - http
  - telegram
  - slack
  - discord
  - whatsapp

# ── Workflow (optional) ───────────────────────────────────────
workflow:
  steps:
    - id: gather
      agent: researcher
      input:
        query: "{{input.message}}"
    - id: write
      agent: writer
      input:
        research: "{{steps.gather.output}}"
        topic: "{{input.message}}"
      depends_on: [gather]

# ── Capabilities ──────────────────────────────────────────────
capabilities:
  - web_search
  - code_execution

# ── Metadata ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
tags:
  - productivity
  - research
author: "Your Name"

docs.title: name (required)
docs.text: Unique identifier for the agent. Used in API paths (/v1/agents/{name}/chat) and log output. Must match [a-z0-9-]+.

docs.title: model (required)
docs.text:
LLM model to use. Use a bare name for the default provider, or prefix with provider/ to select a specific one.

model: gpt-4o-mini                              # openai (default)
model: anthropic/claude-3-5-haiku-20241022      # anthropic
model: ollama/llama3.2                          # ollama (local)
model: together/meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-70B  # together
model: qwen/qwen-max                            # qwen

docs.title: system_prompt
docs.text: The agent's base instructions. Supports multi-line YAML block syntax (|). The system prompt is prepended to every conversation context.

docs.title: tools
docs.text: List of built-in tools the agent can invoke. See Built-in Tools for the full list.

docs.title: token_budget
docs.text: Field Default Description max_input_tokens provider max Maximum tokens in the context window sent to the LLM max_output_tokens provider max Maximum tokens the LLM may generate

docs.title: session.history_turns
docs.text: Number of prior conversation turns to include in context. Default: 20. Set to 0 to disable memory (stateless agent).

docs.title: channels
docs.text: Which messaging channels this agent is accessible on. Options: http, telegram, slack, discord, whatsapp.

docs.title: workflow
docs.text: Defines a multi-step DAG pipeline. See Workflow DAGs.

docs.title: capabilities
docs.text: Declares what this agent can do — used by the capability gap detector to surface missing configuration at startup.

docs.title: tags / author
docs.text: Metadata for the agent marketplace. Optional.

docs.title: Built-in Tools
docs.text: Agents can be equipped with tools that extend their capabilities beyond text generation. Tools are declared in SOUL.yaml and invoked autonomously by the LLM.

docs.title: Available tools
docs.text: Tool Description web_search Search the web via DuckDuckGo url_fetch Fetch and extract text from a URL calculator Evaluate mathematical expressions code_interpreter Execute Python code in a sandbox image_analyze Analyze an image passed in the conversation

docs.title: web_search
docs.text:
Searches the web and returns a summary of the top results.

tools:
  - web_search

Parameters (set by the LLM automatically):

Parameter Type Description query string The search query num_results int Number of results (default: 5)

Example LLM invocation:

{
  "tool": "web_search",
  "query": "latest news on AI agents 2025",
  "num_results": 3
}

docs.title: url_fetch
docs.text:
Fetches a URL and returns the cleaned text content. Useful for reading articles, documentation, or API responses.

tools:
  - url_fetch
Parameter Type Description url string URL to fetch max_chars int Truncate response to this many characters (default: 8000)

docs.title: calculator
docs.text:
Evaluates a mathematical expression safely.

tools:
  - calculator
Parameter Type Description expression string Math expression to evaluate (e.g. "2^10 + sqrt(144)")

Supports: arithmetic, powers, square roots, trigonometry, logarithms.

docs.title: code_interpreter
docs.text:
Executes Python code in an isolated sandbox and returns stdout. Useful for data processing, calculations, and file manipulation.

tools:
  - code_interpreter
Parameter Type Description code string Python code to execute timeout int Execution timeout in seconds (default: 10)

Sandbox

Code runs in a restricted subprocess with no network access and limited filesystem. Output is capped at 4096 characters.

docs.title: image_analyze
docs.text:
Analyzes an image passed as a base64 data URI or URL (requires a vision-capable model such as gpt-4o).

model: gpt-4o
tools:
  - image_analyze
Parameter Type Description image_url string URL or data:image/...;base64,... prompt string What to analyze or extract

docs.title: Combining tools
docs.text:
Tools compose naturally. An agent with both web_search and url_fetch can search, pick the best result, fetch its full text, and summarize — all in one turn:

name: deep-researcher
model: gpt-4o
system_prompt: |
  You are a thorough researcher. Search for information, fetch relevant pages,
  and provide well-cited summaries.
tools:
  - web_search
  - url_fetch
  - calculator
channels:
  - http

docs.title: Workflow DAGs
docs.text: Workflows let you chain multiple agents into multi-step pipelines with typed inputs, outputs, and dependency resolution.

docs.title: Concepts
docs.text:
• Step — a single agent invocation within the workflow
  • DAG — directed acyclic graph of steps; Soulacy resolves the execution order automatically
  • Template variables — {{input.*}} and {{steps.id.output}} for passing data between steps

docs.title: Basic example
docs.text:
agents/report-writer.soul.yaml
name: report-writer
description: Research a topic and produce a polished report
model: gpt-4o
system_prompt: Orchestrate the research and writing pipeline.
channels:
  - http

workflow:
  steps:
    - id: research
      agent: researcher
      input:
        query: "{{input.message}}"

    - id: outline
      agent: outliner
      input:
        research_notes: "{{steps.research.output}}"
      depends_on: [research]

    - id: write
      agent: writer
      input:
        outline: "{{steps.outline.output}}"
        research: "{{steps.research.output}}"
      depends_on: [outline]

When a user messages report-writer, the pipeline runs:

  1. researcher runs with the user's message as query
  2. outliner runs once researcher completes
  3. writer runs once outliner completes, receiving both the outline and raw research

The final output is the result of the last step (write).

docs.title: Parallel steps
docs.text:
Steps without depends_on (or with the same dependencies) run in parallel:

workflow:
  steps:
    - id: search_web
      agent: web-researcher
      input:
        query: "{{input.message}}"

    - id: search_docs
      agent: doc-searcher
      input:
        query: "{{input.message}}"

    # Both search_web and search_docs run in parallel.
    # synthesizer waits for both.
    - id: synthesize
      agent: synthesizer
      input:
        web_results: "{{steps.search_web.output}}"
        doc_results: "{{steps.search_docs.output}}"
      depends_on: [search_web, search_docs]

docs.title: Template variables
docs.text: Variable Description {{input.message}} The original user message {{input.session_id}} The session ID {{input.agent_id}} The workflow agent's ID {{steps.<id>.output}} The output of a completed step

docs.title: Error handling
docs.text:
By default, if any step fails, the workflow fails and returns an error. Failed steps are pushed to the dead-letter queue for inspection.

workflow:
  steps:
    - id: risky-step
      agent: external-api-caller
      input:
        url: "{{input.message}}"
      retry:
        max_attempts: 3
        backoff: exponential
        initial_delay: 1s

docs.title: Retry policy
docs.text:
retry:
  max_attempts: 3          # default: 1
  backoff: exponential     # linear | exponential | fixed
  initial_delay: 500ms
  max_delay: 30s

docs.title: Step timeout
docs.text:
- id: slow-step
  agent: data-processor
  input:
    data: "{{input.message}}"
  timeout: 60s

docs.title: Viewing workflow runs
docs.text: Workflow execution produces OTEL traces — each step appears as a child span. View in Jaeger or any OTLP-compatible backend.

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text: Soulacy exposes a REST API on the configured port (default 8080). All endpoints require authentication unless noted.

docs.title: Base URL
docs.text: http://localhost:8080/v1

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
Include an auth token in every request:

Authorization: Bearer <token>

Accepted token types: server API key (sy_), managed API key (sk_), or JWT (eyJ). See Auth.

docs.title: Content type
docs.text: Content-Type: application/json

docs.title: Response format
docs.text:
All responses are JSON. Errors follow this shape:

{
  "error": "unauthorized",
  "message": "invalid or expired token",
  "status": 401
}

docs.text: Method Path Description POST /v1/agents/{id}/chat Chat with an agent GET /v1/agents List agents GET /v1/agents/{id} Get agent details POST /v1/auth/login Issue a JWT POST /v1/admin/api-keys Create a managed API key DELETE /v1/admin/api-keys/{id} Revoke an API key GET /v1/credentials List credentials POST /v1/credentials Store a credential DELETE /v1/credentials/{id} Delete a credential GET /v1/costs Cost summary GET /v1/costs/breakdown Per-agent cost breakdown GET /v1/admin/dlq Dead-letter queue POST /v1/admin/dlq/{id}/retry Retry a DLQ item GET /v1/health Health check (no auth required)

docs.title: Streaming
docs.text:
Add Accept: text/event-stream to receive token-by-token streaming via Server-Sent Events.

curl -N -X POST http://localhost:8080/v1/agents/assistant/chat \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer sk_..." \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Accept: text/event-stream" \
  -d '{"message": "Tell me a joke", "session_id": "u1"}'

Events:

data: {"delta": "Why"}
data: {"delta": " don't"}
data: {"delta": " scientists"}
...
data: {"done": true, "tokens_used": {"input": 8, "output": 22}}

The plaintext value is never returned after creation.

docs.text:
Reference a stored credential by name in agent configuration:

name: my-agent
model: gpt-4o
credentials:
  openai_key: openai-prod   # credential name from the vault

At runtime, Soulacy substitutes the decrypted value before passing it to the LLM provider.

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text: Soulacy is a single Go binary with a layered architecture. This page describes how components fit together.

docs.title: Component map
docs.text: Package Path Responsibility HTTP server cmd/soulacy/ Entry point, dependency wiring Auth engine internal/auth/ Middleware chain, JWT, API keys, RBAC Runtime engine internal/runtime/ Agent dispatch, history, DLQ LLM client internal/llm/ Provider adapters (OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama…) Channel adapters internal/channels/ Telegram, Slack, Discord, WhatsApp, HTTP Session history internal/session/ Conversation history store Dead-letter queue internal/dlq/ Failed message persistence API keys internal/auth/apikeys/ Managed sk_ key lifecycle Credential vault internal/vault/ AES-256-GCM encrypted secrets Rate limiter internal/ratelimit/ Per-user/org sliding window Cost tracker internal/costs/ Token usage recording Telemetry internal/telemetry/ OTEL traces + Prometheus metrics Agent builder internal/builder/ SOUL.yaml parsing, validation, capability gap detection Workflow executor internal/workflow/ DAG resolution and parallel step execution Marketplace internal/marketplace/ Agent registry and install

docs.title: Request lifecycle
docs.text:
A typical HTTP API request flows through:

  1. Fiber HTTP server receives POST /v1/agents/assistant/chat
  2. Auth middleware validates the bearer token (static key → sk_ key → JWT)
  3. Rate limiter middleware checks per-user request and token quotas
  4. Runtime engine looks up the agent definition and builds the context (system prompt + history)
  5. LLM client calls the configured provider with the assembled context
  6. Tool runner handles any tool calls the LLM emits (web_search, etc.) in a loop
  7. History store appends the user and assistant turns to the conversation log
  8. Cost tracker records token usage against the user/agent/org
  9. OTEL emits a trace span covering the entire request
  10. Response is returned to the caller

If any step after auth panics or times out, the deferred DLQ handler pushes the message to the dead-letter queue for later inspection or retry.

docs.title: Design principles
docs.text:
Single binary — Soulacy compiles to a single self-contained binary with no runtime dependencies. Deploy it anywhere Go runs.

Local interfaces — Packages define minimal interfaces for their dependencies rather than importing concrete types. This keeps the dependency graph acyclic and makes testing straightforward.

Pluggable storage — The storage layer is abstracted behind interfaces. SQLite, Postgres, and in-memory backends are interchangeable.

Layered auth — Auth is checked once at the middleware layer. Downstream components receive a validated Claims struct and never touch raw tokens.

Structured logging — All logs use zap in JSON mode. Log level, correlation IDs, and trace context are consistent across components.

Graceful shutdown — The server handles SIGINT/SIGTERM, drains in-flight requests up to a configurable timeout, and closes storage connections cleanly.

docs.title: Channels Overview
docs.text: Channels are adapters that connect agents to messaging platforms. Each channel handles authentication, message formatting, and webhook delivery for its platform.

docs.title: Supported channels
docs.text: Channel Status Config key HTTP ✅ Stable — (always active) Telegram ✅ Stable channels.telegram Slack ✅ Stable channels.slack Discord ✅ Stable channels.discord WhatsApp ✅ Stable channels.whatsapp

docs.title: How channels work
docs.text:
1. A user sends a message on a platform (Telegram, Slack, etc.)
  2. The platform delivers a webhook to Soulacy
  3. The channel adapter normalises the message into a Message struct
  4. The runtime engine dispatches to the correct agent
  5. The agent produces a reply
  6. The channel adapter formats and sends the reply back

All channels share the same agent engine and conversation history — a user can switch channels mid-conversation and context is preserved.

docs.title: Multi-channel agents
docs.text:
An agent can be available on multiple channels simultaneously:

agents/assistant.soul.yaml
name: assistant
model: gpt-4o-mini
system_prompt: You are a helpful assistant.
channels:
  - http
  - telegram
  - slack
  - discord

docs.title: Channel-specific formatting
docs.text:
Each channel adapter handles platform-specific formatting automatically:

  • Telegram — Markdown → MarkdownV2 escaping, inline buttons
  • Slack — Markdown → Block Kit, thread replies
  • Discord — Markdown → Discord markdown, embed cards
  • WhatsApp — Plain text (WhatsApp does not support rich formatting)
  • HTTP — Raw text or JSON, caller decides rendering

docs.title: Discord
docs.text: Connect agents to Discord using the Discord Gateway (WebSocket).

docs.title: 1. Create a Discord application
docs.text:
1. Go to discord.com/developers/applications
  2. Click New Application, give it a name
  3. Navigate to Bot → Add Bot
  4. Copy the Token

docs.title: 2. Set bot permissions
docs.text:
Under OAuth2 → URL Generator, select:

  • Scopes: bot, applications.commands
  • Bot Permissions: Send Messages, Read Message History, View Channels

docs.title: 3. Configure intents
docs.text:
Under Bot → Privileged Gateway Intents, enable:

  • Message Content Intent (required to read message text)

docs.title: 4. Invite the bot to your server
docs.text: Use the generated OAuth2 URL to add the bot to your server.

docs.title: 5. Configure Soulacy
docs.text:
config.yaml
channels:
  discord:
    token: "MTI3..."    # Bot token
    agents:
      - assistant
    default_agent: assistant

docs.title: Routing by channel
docs.text:
channels:
  discord:
    token: "..."
    routes:
      "support": support-agent      # channel name
      "research": researcher
    default_agent: assistant

docs.title: Features
docs.text: Feature Support Direct messages ✅ Server channel messages ✅ (mention the bot) Thread replies ✅ Embeds ✅ Slash commands 🔜 Planned Attachments ✅ (images passed to vision agents)

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• The bot only responds when directly mentioned (@BotName) in a server channel to avoid noise
  • In DMs, all messages are processed without needing a mention
  • Discord has a 2000-character message limit; long responses are automatically split across multiple messages

docs.title: HTTP API Channel
docs.text: The HTTP channel is always active and exposes a REST API for direct integration with web apps, mobile apps, and other services.

docs.title: Chat endpoint
docs.text:
POST /v1/agents/{agent_id}/chat
Authorization: Bearer <token>
Content-Type: application/json

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
{
  "message": "What is the capital of France?",
  "session_id": "user-123",        // optional; omit to start a new session
  "attachments": [                  // optional
    {
      "type": "image",
      "url": "https://example.com/photo.jpg"
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "reply": "The capital of France is Paris.",
  "agent_id": "assistant",
  "session_id": "user-123",
  "tokens_used": {
    "input": 42,
    "output": 12
  }
}

docs.title: Streaming (SSE)
docs.text:
For real-time token streaming, add Accept: text/event-stream:

curl -N -X POST http://localhost:8080/v1/agents/assistant/chat \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer sy_your-key" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Accept: text/event-stream" \
  -d '{"message": "Tell me a short story", "session_id": "u1"}'

Events:

data: {"delta": "Once"}
data: {"delta": " upon"}
data: {"delta": " a time"}
data: {"done": true, "session_id": "u1", "tokens_used": {"input": 12, "output": 24}}

docs.title: Session management
docs.text:
Sessions persist conversation history. Use the same session_id across requests to maintain context:

# Turn 1
curl -X POST .../agents/assistant/chat \
  -d '{"message": "My name is Alice", "session_id": "alice-session"}'

# Turn 2 — agent remembers the name
curl -X POST .../agents/assistant/chat \
  -d '{"message": "What is my name?", "session_id": "alice-session"}'
# Response: "Your name is Alice."

Sessions are stored in the configured storage backend and preserved across server restarts.

docs.title: Attachments
docs.text:
Pass images or documents in the request:

{
  "message": "What does this chart show?",
  "session_id": "u1",
  "attachments": [
    {
      "type": "image",
      "url": "https://example.com/chart.png"
    }
  ]
}

Requires a vision-capable model (gpt-4o, claude-3-5-sonnet, etc.) configured for the agent.

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
The HTTP channel respects the full auth stack:

Token type Header Server API key Authorization: Bearer sy_... Managed API key Authorization: Bearer sk_... JWT Authorization: Bearer eyJ...

See Auth configuration for details.

docs.title: Slack
docs.text: Connect agents to Slack using the Slack Events API and Socket Mode.

docs.title: 1. Create a Slack app
docs.text:
1. Go to api.slack.com/apps → Create New App → From scratch
  2. Choose a name and workspace

docs.title: 2. Configure OAuth scopes
docs.text:
Under OAuth & Permissions → Bot Token Scopes, add:

  • app_mentions:read
  • channels:history
  • chat:write
  • files:read (optional, for document handling)
  • im:history
  • im:write

docs.title: 3. Enable Socket Mode (recommended)
docs.text: Under Socket Mode, enable it and generate an App-Level Token with connections:write scope. This avoids needing a public webhook URL.

docs.title: 4. Enable Events
docs.text:
Under Event Subscriptions, enable and subscribe to:

  • app_mention
  • message.im

docs.title: 5. Install to workspace and copy tokens
docs.text:
• Bot Token (xoxb-...) — from OAuth & Permissions
  • App-Level Token (xapp-...) — from Socket Mode (if using socket mode)
  • Signing Secret — from Basic Information

docs.title: 6. Configure Soulacy
docs.text:
config.yaml
channels:
  slack:
    bot_token: "xoxb-..."
    app_token: "xapp-..."        # Socket Mode token
    signing_secret: "..."
    agents:
      - assistant
    default_agent: assistant

docs.title: Webhook mode (alternative)
docs.text:
If you prefer webhooks over Socket Mode:

channels:
  slack:
    bot_token: "xoxb-..."
    signing_secret: "..."
    mode: webhook
    webhook_path: /webhooks/slack
    agents:
      - assistant

Set the Request URL in Event Subscriptions to https://yourdomain.com/webhooks/slack.

docs.title: Features
docs.text: Feature Support DM messages ✅ Channel mentions ✅ Thread replies ✅ (agent replies in thread) Block Kit formatting ✅ File uploads ✅ (text extracted) Slash commands 🔜 Planned

docs.title: Routing by channel
docs.text:
channels:
  slack:
    bot_token: "xoxb-..."
    app_token: "xapp-..."
    signing_secret: "..."
    routes:
      "#support": support-agent
      "#research": researcher
    default_agent: assistant

Messages in #support are routed to the support-agent, messages in #research to researcher, and DMs to assistant.

docs.title: Telegram
docs.text: Connect your agents to Telegram using the Bot API.

docs.title: 1. Create a bot
docs.text:
1. Open Telegram and message @BotFather
  2. Send /newbot and follow the prompts
  3. Copy the token: 1234567890:AAH...

docs.title: 2. Configure Soulacy
docs.text:
config.yaml
channels:
  telegram:
    token: "1234567890:AAH..."
    agents:
      - assistant          # which agents are reachable on Telegram
    webhook_url: "https://yourdomain.com/webhooks/telegram"   # optional

docs.title: 3. Webhook vs polling
docs.text:
Polling (default) — Soulacy polls Telegram for new updates. Works without a public URL. Best for development.

Webhook — Telegram pushes updates to your server. Requires a publicly reachable HTTPS URL. Best for production.

channels:
  telegram:
    token: "..."
    mode: webhook                            # polling (default) | webhook
    webhook_url: "https://yourdomain.com/webhooks/telegram"

On startup, Soulacy automatically registers the webhook URL with Telegram.

docs.title: Multi-agent routing
docs.text:
Route different Telegram commands to different agents:

channels:
  telegram:
    token: "..."
    routes:
      /start: assistant
      /research: researcher
      /summary: summarizer
    default_agent: assistant    # fallback for messages without a command

docs.title: Features
docs.text: Feature Support Text messages ✅ Photos / images ✅ (passed to vision-capable agents) Documents ✅ (text extracted and passed as context) Voice messages 🔜 Planned Inline keyboards ✅ (agent can emit buttons) Groups & channels ✅ (mention the bot to trigger) Commands ✅

docs.title: Example: minimal Telegram bot
docs.text:
config.yaml
server:
  api_key: "sy_..."
llm:
  default_provider: openai
  providers:
    openai:
      api_key: "sk-..."
storage:
  type: sqlite
  path: ./soulacy.db
channels:
  telegram:
    token: "1234567890:AAH..."
    agents: [assistant]
agents:
  dir: ./agents
agents/assistant.soul.yaml
name: assistant
model: gpt-4o-mini
system_prompt: You are a helpful assistant on Telegram.
channels:
  - telegram

Start with soulacy serve --config config.yaml and message your bot on Telegram.

docs.title: WhatsApp
docs.text: Connect agents to WhatsApp using the WhatsApp Business Platform (Meta Cloud API).

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• A Meta Business Account
  • A WhatsApp Business Account linked to it
  • A phone number approved for WhatsApp Business API

Note: WhatsApp Business API is not free — it is billed per conversation. See Meta's pricing.

docs.title: 1. Create a Meta App
docs.text:
1. Go to developers.facebook.com → My Apps → Create App
  2. Select Business app type
  3. Add the WhatsApp product

docs.title: 2. Configure the phone number
docs.text: In the WhatsApp section of your app, add and verify your business phone number.

docs.title: 3. Get credentials
docs.text:
• Access Token — permanent token from Meta Business Suite
  • Phone Number ID — the ID of your WhatsApp phone number
  • Webhook Verify Token — any secret string you choose

docs.title: 4. Configure Soulacy
docs.text:
config.yaml
channels:
  whatsapp:
    access_token: "EAA..."
    phone_number_id: "123456789"
    verify_token: "my-webhook-secret"
    webhook_path: /webhooks/whatsapp
    agents:
      - assistant
    default_agent: assistant

docs.title: 5. Register the webhook
docs.text:
In your Meta app under WhatsApp → Configuration, set:

  • Callback URL: https://yourdomain.com/webhooks/whatsapp
  • Verify Token: the same string as verify_token above
  • Subscribe to: messages

docs.title: Features
docs.text: Feature Support Text messages ✅ Images ✅ (passed to vision agents) Documents ✅ (text extracted) Audio 🔜 Planned Rich formatting ❌ (WhatsApp is plain text only) Templates 🔜 Planned

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• WhatsApp does not support markdown, bold, or links in outgoing messages — responses are sent as plain text
  • The business phone number cannot receive regular WhatsApp messages once it's on the API
  • 24-hour conversation window applies: you can only reply within 24 hours of the last user message (or use approved message templates)

docs.title: CLI Reference
docs.text: The soulacy binary provides several commands.

docs.title: Global flags
docs.text: Flag Default Description --config ./config.yaml Path to config file --log-level info Log level: debug, info, warn, error --log-format json Log format: json, text

docs.title: soulacy serve
docs.text:
Start the Soulacy server.

soulacy serve [flags]
Flag Default Description --config ./config.yaml Config file path --host from config Override server host --port from config Override server port --dev false Enable development mode (pretty logs, no rate limits)

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Start with default config
soulacy serve

# Custom config path
soulacy serve --config /etc/soulacy/config.yaml

# Development mode
soulacy serve --dev --log-format text

docs.title: soulacy validate
docs.text:
Validate a config file and all SOUL.yaml files without starting the server.

soulacy validate --config config.yaml

Output:

✓ config.yaml — valid
✓ agents/assistant.soul.yaml — valid
✓ agents/researcher.soul.yaml — valid
✗ agents/broken.soul.yaml — unknown field: modell (did you mean model?)

docs.title: soulacy agent
docs.text: Manage agents from the CLI.

docs.title: List agents
docs.text:
soulacy agent list --config config.yaml

Output:

ID           MODEL          CHANNELS          TOOLS
assistant    gpt-4o-mini    http,telegram     -
researcher   gpt-4o         http              web_search,url_fetch

docs.title: Chat with an agent (REPL)
docs.text:
soulacy agent chat assistant --config config.yaml

Starts an interactive REPL session in the terminal:

Session: sess_abc123
Type your message and press Enter. Ctrl+C to exit.

> Hello!
assistant: Hi! How can I help you today?

> What's 2 + 2?
assistant: 4.

docs.title: soulacy keys
docs.text: Manage API keys.

docs.title: Create a key
docs.text:
soulacy keys create --name ci-bot --role operator --config config.yaml

Output:

API Key created:
  ID:   ak_abc123
  Name: ci-bot
  Role: operator
  Key:  sk_xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx  ← save this, shown once

docs.title: List keys
docs.text: soulacy keys list --config config.yaml

docs.title: Revoke a key
docs.text: soulacy keys revoke ak_abc123 --config config.yaml

docs.title: soulacy costs
docs.text:
View cost summaries from the CLI.

# 30-day summary
soulacy costs summary --config config.yaml

# Per-agent breakdown
soulacy costs breakdown --config config.yaml --period 7d

# Filter by agent
soulacy costs breakdown --agent researcher --period 30d

docs.title: soulacy version
docs.text:
Print version and build information.

soulacy version
# Soulacy v0.1.0 (go1.25 linux/amd64) built 2026-05-28

docs.title: Configuration Overview
docs.text:
Soulacy is configured via a single config.yaml file. Pass its path with --config:

soulacy serve --config /etc/soulacy/config.yaml

docs.title: Full structure
docs.text:
server:        # HTTP server settings
  host: 0.0.0.0
  port: 8080
  api_key: "..."

llm:           # LLM provider registry
  default_provider: openai
  providers: { ... }

storage:       # Persistence backend
  type: sqlite
  path: ./soulacy.db

auth:          # Authentication & RBAC
  jwt_secret: "..."
  api_keys: { ... }

channels:      # Messaging channel adapters
  telegram: { ... }
  slack: { ... }
  discord: { ... }
  whatsapp: { ... }

rate_limit:    # Per-user/org rate limiting
  enabled: true
  ...

telemetry:     # OpenTelemetry & cost tracking
  enabled: true
  ...

agents:        # Agent discovery
  dir: ./agents

docs.title: Environment variable overrides
docs.text:
Any config key can be overridden with an environment variable using the pattern SOULACY__SECTION__KEY:

export SOULACY__SERVER__API_KEY="my-secret"
export SOULACY__LLM__PROVIDERS__OPENAI__API_KEY="sk-..."

docs.title: Sections
docs.text:
Section Description server Host, port, API key, TLS llm LLM providers and defaults storage SQLite, Postgres, Redis auth JWT, managed API keys, RBAC rate_limit Per-user and per-org quotas telemetry OTEL traces and cost tracking

Keep secrets out of version control

Your config.yaml contains API keys and tokens. Add it to .gitignore and use environment variable overrides or a secrets manager in production.

docs.title: Auth & Security
docs.text: Soulacy has a layered authentication system: static bearer tokens, managed API keys, and JWTs — all checked in sequence by the auth middleware.

docs.title: Reference
docs.text:
auth:
  # JWT signing secret (HS256)
  jwt_secret: "your-jwt-secret-min-32-chars"
  jwt_expiry: 24h

  # Master server key (same as server.api_key, checked first)
  # Configured under server.api_key

  # Managed API keys (sk_ prefix)
  api_keys:
    enabled: true
    # Keys are stored hashed in the database.
    # Create via POST /v1/admin/api-keys

  # RBAC roles
  roles:
    - name: admin
      permissions: ["*"]
    - name: operator
      permissions: ["agents:read", "agents:invoke", "costs:read"]
    - name: viewer
      permissions: ["agents:read"]

docs.title: Authentication flow
docs.text:
Requests are authenticated in this order:

  1. Static server API key — Authorization: Bearer sy_... Full admin access. Matches server.api_key exactly.

  2. Managed API key — Authorization: Bearer sk_... Scoped keys stored in the database. Role is assigned at key creation time.

  3. JWT — Authorization: Bearer eyJ... Short-lived tokens issued by POST /v1/auth/login. Carry user identity, email, and role.

If none match, the request is rejected with 401 Unauthorized.

docs.title: JWT
docs.text:
Issue a JWT by calling the login endpoint with valid credentials:

curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/v1/auth/login \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"email": "user@example.com", "password": "..."}'

JWTs expire after jwt_expiry (default 24h). Configure a strong secret:

openssl rand -hex 32

docs.title: Managed API keys
docs.text:
Managed API keys (sk_ prefix) are created via the admin API and stored as bcrypt hashes — the plaintext is shown only once at creation time.

# Create a key with operator role
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/v1/admin/api-keys \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer sy_your-server-key" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"name": "ci-bot", "role": "operator"}'

# Response
{
  "id": "ak_abc123",
  "key": "sk_xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx",   ← shown only once
  "role": "operator"
}

docs.title: RBAC roles
docs.text:
Role Permissions admin All operations operator Invoke agents, read costs, manage credentials viewer Read agents and metadata only

Roles are assigned at key or token creation time and embedded in the JWT claims.

docs.title: Credential vault
docs.text: Sensitive credentials (LLM keys, channel tokens, third-party secrets) can be stored encrypted in the database and referenced by agents at runtime. See the Credentials API.

docs.title: LLM Providers
docs.text: Soulacy supports multiple LLM providers simultaneously. Each agent picks its provider via the model field in SOUL.yaml.

docs.title: Reference
docs.text:
llm:
  default_provider: openai   # used when model has no prefix
  timeout: 120s              # per-request LLM timeout

  providers:
    openai:
      api_key: "sk-..."
      base_url: https://api.openai.com/v1   # override for proxies

    anthropic:
      api_key: "sk-ant-..."

    ollama:
      base_url: http://localhost:11434

    together:
      api_key: "..."
      base_url: https://api.together.xyz/v1

    qwen:
      api_key: "..."
      base_url: https://dashscope.aliyuncs.com/compatible-mode/v1

docs.title: Selecting a model in SOUL.yaml
docs.text:
Use bare model names for the default provider, or prefix with provider/:

# Uses default_provider (openai)
model: gpt-4o-mini

# Explicit provider prefix
model: anthropic/claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022
model: ollama/llama3.2
model: together/meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct-Turbo
model: qwen/qwen-max

docs.title: Supported providers
docs.text: Provider Config key Notes OpenAI openai GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, o1, o3 Anthropic anthropic Claude 3.5, Claude 3 Haiku Ollama ollama Local models, no API key needed Together AI together Open-source models at scale Qwen (Alibaba) qwen Qwen-Max, Qwen-Plus, Qwen-Turbo

docs.title: Custom / compatible endpoints
docs.text:
Any OpenAI-compatible API (vLLM, LM Studio, Groq, etc.) works via the base_url override:

llm:
  providers:
    openai:
      api_key: "not-needed"
      base_url: http://localhost:1234/v1   # LM Studio

docs.title: Timeouts
docs.text:
llm:
  timeout: 180s   # increase for slow models or long context

Per-agent token budgets are configured in SOUL.yaml.

docs.title: Rate Limiting
docs.text: Soulacy enforces per-user and per-org rate limits to protect costs and prevent abuse.

docs.title: Reference
docs.text:
rate_limit:
  enabled: true

  # Global defaults (apply to all users unless overridden)
  default:
    requests_per_minute: 60
    requests_per_hour: 500
    requests_per_day: 5000
    tokens_per_day: 500000      # LLM tokens consumed

  # Per-role overrides
  roles:
    admin:
      requests_per_minute: 0    # 0 = unlimited
    operator:
      requests_per_minute: 120
      tokens_per_day: 2000000
    viewer:
      requests_per_minute: 30
      tokens_per_day: 100000

  # Per-agent overrides
  agents:
    heavy-agent:
      requests_per_minute: 10

  # Storage backend for counters
  # Uses Redis if configured, otherwise in-process
  backend: redis                # redis | memory

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
Rate limits use a sliding window algorithm. When a request arrives:

  1. The user/org identity is extracted from the JWT or API key.
  2. Current counts are fetched from Redis (or in-memory store).
  3. If any limit is exceeded, 429 Too Many Requests is returned with a Retry-After header.
  4. Otherwise, the request proceeds and counters are incremented.

docs.title: Response headers
docs.text:
Every API response includes current limit headers:

X-RateLimit-Limit: 60
X-RateLimit-Remaining: 42
X-RateLimit-Reset: 1717000000
Retry-After: 15          ← only on 429 responses

docs.title: Token budget tracking
docs.text:
Soulacy tracks LLM token consumption per user and per agent. Token counts are reported by the LLM provider and stored in the cost records table.

Token limits work alongside request limits — a single request with a very large context can exhaust the token budget even if the request count limit has not been reached.

docs.title: Distributed rate limiting
docs.text:
When running multiple Soulacy replicas, configure Redis so all nodes share the same counters:

storage:
  redis:
    addr: redis:6379

rate_limit:
  backend: redis

Without Redis, each replica maintains independent in-memory counters, which allows up to N × limit total requests across N replicas.

docs.title: Disabling rate limits
docs.text:
rate_limit:
  enabled: false

Not recommended in production.

docs.title: Server Configuration
docs.text: Controls the HTTP server that exposes the REST API and channel webhooks.

docs.title: Reference
docs.text:
server:
  host: 0.0.0.0        # bind address (default: 0.0.0.0)
  port: 8080           # port to listen on (default: 8080)
  api_key: "sy_..."    # master server API key (required)
  read_timeout: 30s    # HTTP read timeout
  write_timeout: 60s   # HTTP write timeout
  idle_timeout: 120s   # keep-alive idle timeout
  tls:
    enabled: false
    cert_file: /etc/ssl/certs/soulacy.crt
    key_file:  /etc/ssl/private/soulacy.key

docs.title: host
docs.text: IP address or hostname to bind. Use 0.0.0.0 to accept connections on all interfaces, or 127.0.0.1 to accept localhost only.

docs.title: port
docs.text: TCP port the HTTP server listens on. Default: 8080.

docs.title: api_key
docs.text:
The master API key for the server. Clients must send this in the Authorization: Bearer <key> header to access protected endpoints. Generate a strong random value:

openssl rand -hex 32

docs.title: tls
docs.text: Enable HTTPS. Provide paths to your certificate and private key files. Behind a reverse proxy (nginx, Caddy, Cloudflare), TLS termination is typically handled externally — leave tls.enabled: false.

docs.title: Example: local dev
docs.text:
server:
  host: 127.0.0.1
  port: 8080
  api_key: "dev-only-key"

docs.title: Example: production with TLS
docs.text:
server:
  host: 0.0.0.0
  port: 443
  api_key: "sy_<strong-random-value>"
  tls:
    enabled: true
    cert_file: /etc/letsencrypt/live/example.com/fullchain.pem
    key_file:  /etc/letsencrypt/live/example.com/privkey.pem

docs.title: Storage
docs.text: Soulacy uses a pluggable storage backend for conversation history, session data, the dead-letter queue, and agent cost records.

docs.title: Reference
docs.text:
storage:
  type: sqlite          # sqlite | postgres | memory
  path: ./soulacy.db   # SQLite only

  # Postgres (when type: postgres)
  postgres:
    dsn: "postgres://user:pass@localhost:5432/soulacy?sslmode=disable"
    max_open_conns: 25
    max_idle_conns: 5
    conn_max_lifetime: 5m

  # Redis — used for rate limit counters and distributed locks
  redis:
    addr: localhost:6379
    password: ""
    db: 0

docs.title: SQLite (default)
docs.text:
Zero-dependency, single-file database. Best for local dev, single-node deployments, and low-traffic bots.

storage:
  type: sqlite
  path: /var/lib/soulacy/soulacy.db

SQLite is WAL-mode enabled automatically for better concurrent read performance.

docs.title: Postgres
docs.text:
Recommended for multi-replica or high-traffic deployments.

storage:
  type: postgres
  postgres:
    dsn: "postgres://soulacy:secret@db:5432/soulacy?sslmode=require"
    max_open_conns: 25

Soulacy runs migrations automatically on startup — no migrate step needed.

docs.title: Memory
docs.text:
In-memory store with no persistence. Data is lost on restart. Useful for testing.

storage:
  type: memory

docs.title: Redis
docs.text:
Redis is optional and used only for:

  • Rate limit counters (distributed, across multiple nodes)
  • Distributed locks

If Redis is not configured, rate limiting falls back to in-process counters (suitable for single-node).

storage:
  redis:
    addr: redis:6379
    password: "your-redis-password"

docs.title: What is stored
docs.text: Data Table / Key Conversation history conversation_entries Session metadata sessions API keys (hashed) api_keys Dead-letter queue dead_letters Cost records cost_records Agent marketplace agent_registry

docs.title: Telemetry
docs.text: Soulacy emits OpenTelemetry traces and metrics, and tracks LLM cost per agent, user, and org.

docs.title: Reference
docs.text:
telemetry:
  enabled: true

  # OpenTelemetry trace export
  otel:
    exporter: otlp          # otlp | jaeger | stdout | none
    endpoint: http://localhost:4318   # OTLP HTTP endpoint
    service_name: soulacy
    sample_rate: 1.0        # 1.0 = 100%, 0.1 = 10%

  # Prometheus metrics
  metrics:
    enabled: true
    path: /metrics          # scrape endpoint

  # Cost tracking
  cost:
    enabled: true
    # Cost records are stored in the database.
    # View via GET /v1/costs

docs.title: Traces
docs.text:
Every agent invocation produces an OTEL trace with spans for:

  • HTTP request handling
  • Auth middleware
  • Agent engine dispatch
  • LLM provider call (including model, token counts, latency)
  • Tool calls (web_search, url_fetch, etc.)
  • Channel adapter send

docs.title: Jaeger (local dev)
docs.text:
docker run -d \
  -p 16686:16686 \
  -p 4318:4318 \
  jaegertracing/all-in-one:latest

telemetry:
  otel:
    exporter: otlp
    endpoint: http://localhost:4318

Open http://localhost:16686 to view traces.

docs.title: Stdout (debug)
docs.text:
telemetry:
  otel:
    exporter: stdout

Prints trace JSON to the server log — useful for debugging without a collector.

docs.title: Metrics
docs.text:
Prometheus metrics are available at /metrics:

Metric Type Description soulacy_requests_total Counter Total HTTP requests by method, path, status soulacy_agent_invocations_total Counter Agent calls by agent ID and status soulacy_llm_tokens_total Counter LLM tokens consumed by provider and model soulacy_llm_latency_seconds Histogram LLM response latency soulacy_rate_limit_hits_total Counter Rate limit rejections by user

docs.title: Cost tracking
docs.text:
Soulacy records token usage and estimated cost for every LLM call. Cost per token is derived from provider pricing tables embedded in the binary (updated with each release).

# View cost summary
curl http://localhost:8080/v1/costs \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer sy_your-key"

# Filter by agent
curl "http://localhost:8080/v1/costs?agent_id=assistant&period=7d"

See the Costs API reference for full details.

docs.title: Disabling telemetry
docs.text:
telemetry:
  enabled: false

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
docker run -d \
  --name soulacy \
  -p 8080:8080 \
  -v $(pwd)/config.yaml:/app/config.yaml \
  -v $(pwd)/agents:/app/agents \
  -v $(pwd)/data:/app/data \
  ghcr.io/vmodekurti/soulacy:latest

docs.title: Basic setup (SQLite)
docs.text:
docker-compose.yml
version: "3.9"

services:
  soulacy:
    image: ghcr.io/vmodekurti/soulacy:latest
    restart: unless-stopped
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"
    volumes:
      - ./config.yaml:/app/config.yaml:ro
      - ./agents:/app/agents:ro
      - soulacy_data:/app/data
    environment:
      - SOULACY__SERVER__PORT=8080

volumes:
  soulacy_data:

docs.title: Full stack (Postgres + Redis + Jaeger)
docs.text:
docker-compose.yml
version: "3.9"

services:
  soulacy:
    image: ghcr.io/vmodekurti/soulacy:latest
    restart: unless-stopped
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"
    volumes:
      - ./config.yaml:/app/config.yaml:ro
      - ./agents:/app/agents:ro
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
      redis:
        condition: service_healthy
    environment:
      - SOULACY__STORAGE__TYPE=postgres
      - SOULACY__STORAGE__POSTGRES__DSN=postgres://soulacy:secret@postgres:5432/soulacy?sslmode=disable
      - SOULACY__STORAGE__REDIS__ADDR=redis:6379

  postgres:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    restart: unless-stopped
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: soulacy
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: secret
      POSTGRES_DB: soulacy
    volumes:
      - pg_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U soulacy"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    restart: unless-stopped
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "redis-cli", "ping"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 5

  jaeger:
    image: jaegertracing/all-in-one:latest
    ports:
      - "16686:16686"    # Jaeger UI
      - "4318:4318"      # OTLP HTTP

volumes:
  pg_data:

docs.title: Environment variable overrides
docs.text:
All config values can be set via environment variables (useful for secrets in CI/CD):

SOULACY__SERVER__API_KEY=sy_secret
SOULACY__LLM__PROVIDERS__OPENAI__API_KEY=sk-...
SOULACY__CHANNELS__TELEGRAM__TOKEN=1234:AAH...

docs.title: Health check
docs.text:
curl http://localhost:8080/v1/health
# {"status":"ok","version":"0.1.0"}

Docker healthcheck in Compose:

healthcheck:
  test: ["CMD", "wget", "-qO-", "http://localhost:8080/v1/health"]
  interval: 30s
  timeout: 10s
  retries: 3

docs.title: Using a reverse proxy (nginx)
Linux / VPS Deployment
docs.text: Deploy Soulacy on a Linux VPS as a systemd service with a Caddy reverse proxy for automatic HTTPS.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Ubuntu 22.04+ / Debian 12+ / any systemd-based distro
  • A domain name pointing to your server's IP

docs.title: Install the binary
docs.text:
# Download latest release
curl -sL https://github.com/vmodekurti/soulacy/releases/latest/download/soulacy_linux_amd64.tar.gz \
  | tar -xz -C /usr/local/bin/

soulacy version

docs.title: Create a system user
docs.text: useradd --system --no-create-home --shell /usr/sbin/nologin soulacy

docs.title: Set up directories
docs.text:
mkdir -p /etc/soulacy /var/lib/soulacy /etc/soulacy/agents
chown -R soulacy:soulacy /var/lib/soulacy /etc/soulacy

docs.title: Create config
docs.text:
cat > /etc/soulacy/config.yaml << 'EOF'
server:
  host: 127.0.0.1
  port: 8080
  api_key: "sy_CHANGE_ME"

llm:
  default_provider: openai
  providers:
    openai:
      api_key: "sk-..."

storage:
  type: sqlite
  path: /var/lib/soulacy/soulacy.db

agents:
  dir: /etc/soulacy/agents
EOF

chown soulacy:soulacy /etc/soulacy/config.yaml
chmod 600 /etc/soulacy/config.yaml   # secrets — readable only by soulacy user

docs.title: Create systemd service
docs.text:
/etc/systemd/system/soulacy.service
[Unit]
Description=Soulacy AI Agent Server
After=network.target
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=soulacy
Group=soulacy
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/soulacy serve --config /etc/soulacy/config.yaml
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5s

# Hardening
NoNewPrivileges=true
PrivateTmp=true
ProtectSystem=strict
ReadWritePaths=/var/lib/soulacy
ReadOnlyPaths=/etc/soulacy

# Logging
StandardOutput=journal
StandardError=journal
SyslogIdentifier=soulacy

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl enable soulacy
systemctl start soulacy
systemctl status soulacy

docs.title: Install Caddy (reverse proxy + automatic TLS)
docs.text:
apt install -y debian-keyring debian-archive-keyring apt-transport-https
curl -1sLf 'https://dl.cloudsmith.io/public/caddy/stable/gpg.key' \
  | gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/caddy-stable-archive-keyring.gpg
curl -1sLf 'https://dl.cloudsmith.io/public/caddy/stable/debian.deb.txt' \
  | tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/caddy-stable.list
apt update && apt install caddy
/etc/caddy/Caddyfile
yourdomain.com {
    reverse_proxy localhost:8080 {
        header_up X-Real-IP {remote_host}
        flush_interval -1    # required for SSE streaming
    }
}

systemctl reload caddy

Caddy automatically provisions and renews a Let's Encrypt TLS certificate.

docs.title: Manage the service
docs.text:
# View live logs
journalctl -u soulacy -f

# Restart after config change
systemctl restart soulacy

# Upgrade binary
curl -sL .../soulacy_linux_amd64.tar.gz | tar -xz -C /usr/local/bin/
systemctl restart soulacy

docs.title: Firewall
docs.text:
ufw allow 22/tcp    # SSH
ufw allow 80/tcp    # HTTP (Caddy redirects to HTTPS)
ufw allow 443/tcp   # HTTPS
ufw enable

Port 8080 should remain closed — Soulacy binds to 127.0.0.1 and traffic arrives via Caddy.

docs.title: macOS Deployment
docs.text: Run Soulacy as a persistent background service on macOS using launchd.

docs.title: Install via Homebrew
docs.text:
brew tap vmodekurti/soulacy
brew install soulacy

docs.title: Configure
docs.text:
Create your config file:

mkdir -p ~/Library/Application\ Support/soulacy
cp /usr/local/etc/soulacy/config.example.yaml \
   ~/Library/Application\ Support/soulacy/config.yaml

Edit the config with your API keys and settings.

docs.title: Run as a launchd service
docs.text:
Create a plist:

~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.soulacy.server.plist
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE plist PUBLIC "-//Apple//DTD PLIST 1.0//EN"
  "http://www.apple.com/DTDs/PropertyList-1.0.dtd">
<plist version="1.0">
<dict>
  <key>Label</key>
  <string>com.soulacy.server</string>

  <key>ProgramArguments</key>
  <array>
    <string>/usr/local/bin/soulacy</string>
    <string>serve</string>
    <string>--config</string>
    <string>/Users/YOUR_USERNAME/Library/Application Support/soulacy/config.yaml</string>
  </array>

  <key>RunAtLoad</key>
  <true/>

  <key>KeepAlive</key>
  <true/>

  <key>StandardOutPath</key>
  <string>/usr/local/var/log/soulacy.log</string>

  <key>StandardErrorPath</key>
  <string>/usr/local/var/log/soulacy.error.log</string>
</dict>
</plist>

Load and start:

launchctl load ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.soulacy.server.plist
launchctl start com.soulacy.server

docs.title: Manage the service
docs.text:
# Check status
launchctl list | grep soulacy

# View logs
tail -f /usr/local/var/log/soulacy.log

# Restart
launchctl stop com.soulacy.server
launchctl start com.soulacy.server

# Unload (stop and disable autostart)
launchctl unload ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.soulacy.server.plist

docs.title: Data location
docs.text: Item Default path Config ~/Library/Application Support/soulacy/config.yaml SQLite DB ~/Library/Application Support/soulacy/soulacy.db Logs /usr/local/var/log/soulacy*.log Agents ~/Library/Application Support/soulacy/agents/

docs.title: Exposing to the internet (for channel webhooks)
docs.text:
Telegram, Slack, Discord, and WhatsApp webhooks require a public HTTPS URL. Options:

  • ngrok (dev): ngrok http 8080 — gives you a temporary public URL
  • Cloudflare Tunnel (recommended for persistent): zero-config secure tunnel, no port forwarding needed
  • Router port forwarding + Let's Encrypt: forward port 443 to your Mac, use Caddy for automatic TLS

docs.title: Cloudflare Tunnel (recommended)
docs.text:
brew install cloudflared
cloudflared tunnel login
cloudflared tunnel create soulacy
cloudflared tunnel route dns soulacy yourdomain.com
cloudflared tunnel run --url http://localhost:8080 soulacy

docs.title: Your First Agent
docs.text: This guide walks through building a more capable agent step by step — one that uses tools, maintains conversation history, and enforces a persona.

docs.title: Minimal agent
docs.text:
Every agent starts as a SOUL.yaml file:

name: helper
description: A simple helper agent
model: gpt-4o-mini
system_prompt: You are a helpful assistant.
channels:
  - http

Place this in your agents/ directory and restart Soulacy.

docs.title: Add a persona
docs.text:
Use system_prompt to shape personality and scope:

name: support-bot
description: Customer support for Acme Corp
model: gpt-4o
system_prompt: |
  You are a friendly support agent for Acme Corp.
  - Only answer questions about Acme products.
  - If you don't know, say so and offer to escalate.
  - Keep replies under 150 words unless the user asks for detail.
  - Never reveal internal pricing or roadmap details.
channels:
  - http
  - telegram

docs.title: Add tools
docs.text:
Tools let your agent call functions — fetch data, run code, search the web:

name: researcher
description: Research assistant with web search
model: gpt-4o
system_prompt: |
  You are a research assistant. Use search to find current information.
tools:
  - web_search
  - url_fetch
channels:
  - http

See the Built-in Tools reference for the full list.

docs.title: Set token limits
docs.text:
Control cost and latency per response:

name: summarizer
model: gpt-4o-mini
system_prompt: Summarize documents concisely.
token_budget:
  max_input_tokens: 16000
  max_output_tokens: 512
channels:
  - http

docs.title: Add memory / session history
docs.text:
Session history is enabled by default when you configure a storage backend. The agent automatically receives the last N turns of conversation context.

To control context window:

name: assistant
model: gpt-4o-mini
system_prompt: You are a helpful assistant.
session:
  history_turns: 20      # how many prior turns to include
channels:
  - http

docs.title: Multi-channel agent
docs.text:
Deploy the same agent to multiple channels simultaneously:

name: concierge
description: Multi-channel concierge
model: gpt-4o
system_prompt: |
  You are a concierge assistant. Be polite and efficient.
channels:
  - http
  - telegram
  - slack
  - discord

Each channel uses its own adapter — the agent code is unchanged.

docs.title: Full example
docs.text:
agents/concierge.soul.yaml
name: concierge
description: Full-featured concierge agent
model: gpt-4o
system_prompt: |
  You are a friendly concierge at Acme Inc.
  Help users with questions, research, and task management.
  Be concise. Use bullet points for lists.

tools:
  - web_search
  - url_fetch
  - calculator

token_budget:
  max_input_tokens: 32000
  max_output_tokens: 1024

session:
  history_turns: 30

channels:
  - http
  - telegram
  - slack

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• SOUL.yaml Reference — complete schema documentation
  • Workflow DAGs — chain agents into pipelines
  • Configuration — server, LLM, and storage options

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text: Requirement Version Go 1.25+ (only if building from source) Docker 24+ (optional) OS macOS 13+, Linux (amd64/arm64), Windows 11

docs.title: Homebrew (macOS — recommended)
docs.text:
brew tap vmodekurti/soulacy
brew install soulacy
soulacy version

docs.title: Docker
docs.text:
# Pull latest
docker pull ghcr.io/vmodekurti/soulacy:latest

# Run with your config
docker run -d \
  --name soulacy \
  -p 8080:8080 \
  -v $(pwd)/config.yaml:/app/config.yaml \
  -v $(pwd)/agents:/app/agents \
  ghcr.io/vmodekurti/soulacy:latest

Prefer Compose? See the Docker deployment guide.

docs.title: Pre-built binaries
docs.text:
1. Go to GitHub Releases
  2. Download the archive for your platform:
  3. soulacy_darwin_arm64.tar.gz — Apple Silicon
  4. soulacy_darwin_amd64.tar.gz — Intel Mac
  5. soulacy_linux_amd64.tar.gz — Linux x86-64
  6. soulacy_linux_arm64.tar.gz — Linux ARM
  7. Extract and move to your $PATH:
tar -xzf soulacy_darwin_arm64.tar.gz
sudo mv soulacy /usr/local/bin/
soulacy version

docs.title: Go install
docs.text:
go install github.com/soulacy/soulacy/cmd/soulacy@latest

Ensure $(go env GOPATH)/bin is on your $PATH.

docs.title: Build from source
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/vmodekurti/soulacy.git
cd soulacy
make build
# binary is at ./bin/soulacy

docs.title: Verify the installation
docs.text:
soulacy version
# Soulacy v0.1.0 (go1.25 darwin/arm64)

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text: Follow the Quick Start to create your first agent.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: Get a working AI agent deployed in under 5 minutes.

docs.title: 1. Create a config file
docs.text:
mkdir my-agents && cd my-agents

Create config.yaml:

config.yaml
server:
  host: 0.0.0.0
  port: 8080
  api_key: "your-server-api-key"  # any secret string

llm:
  default_provider: openai
  providers:
    openai:
      api_key: "sk-..."

storage:
  type: sqlite
  path: ./soulacy.db

agents:
  dir: ./agents

docs.title: 2. Create an agent
docs.text:
mkdir agents

Create agents/assistant.soul.yaml:

agents/assistant.soul.yaml
name: assistant
description: A helpful general-purpose assistant
model: gpt-4o-mini
system_prompt: |
  You are a helpful, concise assistant.
channels:
  - http

docs.title: 3. Start the server
docs.text:
soulacy serve --config config.yaml

You should see:

✓ Loaded agent: assistant
✓ HTTP channel listening on :8080
✓ Soulacy is ready

docs.title: 4. Talk to your agent
docs.text:
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/v1/agents/assistant/chat \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer your-server-api-key" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message": "Hello! What can you do?"}'

Response:

{
  "reply": "Hi! I'm a helpful assistant. I can answer questions, help with writing, analysis, and much more. What would you like to do?",
  "agent_id": "assistant",
  "session_id": "sess_abc123"
}

docs.title: 5. Add a Telegram bot (optional)
docs.text:
Get a bot token from @BotFather, then update your config:

config.yaml
channels:
  telegram:
    token: "1234567890:AAH..."
    agents:
      - assistant

Restart — your agent now replies in Telegram.
